name: Minor in Safety Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), so the minor has no captured plan of study, required core, or elective rules. The 60 referenced courses read as a broad, shared engineering-technology pool: only a few are genuinely safety-focused (CET 423 Construction Safety, CHE 312 Chemical Process Safety, CE 414 Engineered Construction, ENGR 430 Engineering for Quality and Reliability), while most are unrelated (BME 210 biosignal processing, CS 440 Computer Vision, ECE 231/232 circuits, ENGR 423/424 drones and robotics, ENTR entrepreneurship). Without a defined structure the progression a safety-minor student would follow cannot be assessed.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit minor structure with a required safety core (e.g., a hazard-analysis course plus CET 423 and CHE 312) and a curated elective list, removing the non-safety courses that appear pulled from a shared engineering-technology pool.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite chains among the referenced courses are well-formed with consistent grade conditions (e.g., ECE 231 -> 232 -> 431 -> 432, CET 331 -> 435), and only four dangling references (ENGR 211, ENGR 312, GER 200, PHYS 231A), which are placeholders or absent labs. However, the deepest chain of 8 is very deep for a minor and is driven by senior major courses (ECE 432 at depth 8, CET 435 at depth 8); with no requirement groups there is no evidence a minor-only student could reach the safety electives without long major-only prerequisite chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Once the required/elective structure is defined, verify that a non-major can satisfy the chosen safety electives without hidden deep chains, and prefer safety courses with shallow prerequisites (e.g., CET 423, which has none).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data. For a minor this is a meaningful gap, because a minor exists to certify a defined competency to non-majors, and there is no statement of what safety-engineering competency this minor certifies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the safety-engineering minor certifies (e.g., ability to perform hazard identification and risk assessment, apply OSHA/industry safety codes, and evaluate system reliability).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits, and creditReconcileDelta are all null. Per the rubric this null profile is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than proof of imbalance, but as presented a prospective student cannot see how many credits the minor requires or how they are distributed between required and elective safety courses.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement (typically 15-18 credits) and list exactly which safety courses count and how many are required versus elective.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured entry is essentially incomplete: no plan of study (requirementGroups empty), no credit total, no program description, and a course pool that blends a handful of safety courses with many unrelated engineering-technology courses. A reader cannot tell what the safety-engineering minor actually consists of.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a clean minor page with a one-paragraph description, the required and elective safety course list, and total credits, and fix the parsing so only genuine safety-engineering courses appear.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The genuinely safety-related courses in the pool are current and vocational (CET 423 addresses OSHA construction safety, CHE 312 covers chemical process safety and quantitative risk, ENGR 430 covers quality and reliability engineering), so a well-chosen selection is marketable. The score is held to adequate because the safety-discipline coverage is narrow (dominated by construction and chemical-process contexts) and there is no explicit modern system-safety, risk-analytics, or fire-protection content, and no structure guaranteeing a student reaches the safety courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended modern safety track (system safety and quantitative risk assessment, plus an EHS or reliability option) so students deliberately acquire in-demand safety-engineering skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled course descriptions are present, specific, and substantive: CHE 312 details chemical-plant accident impact and societal/individual risk, CET 423 cites OSHA compliance, and ENGR 430 explains quality and reliability tools. None read as thin placeholders; only excerpts are truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites on the referenced courses are stated clearly with explicit grade conditions and sensible OR-alternatives (e.g., ENGR 424 'BME 210 or CS 101 or CS 106 or CS 113 or CS 115', COM 313 with ENGL 102 and a GER condition). The main caveat is that several chains reach depth 8, which is deep for a minor and unexplained by the absent structure.

missingTopics.topic: System safety engineering and hazard analysis
missingTopics.rationale: The pool has CHE 312 and CET 423 but no dedicated course on systematic hazard analysis (HAZOP, FMEA, fault-tree/event-tree analysis) or probabilistic risk assessment, which is the defining methodology of safety engineering.
Only CET 423 covers OSHA, and only in a construction context; a general industrial/occupational safety and health course is absent from the captured pool.
A core safety-engineering subfield is entirely missing from the pool, leaving no coverage of fire dynamics, suppression, or explosion prevention.

missingTopics.topic: Ergonomics and human factors in safety
missingTopics.rationale: The pool surfaces no human-factors safety course even though NJIT offers IE 355 Human Factors; occupational injury prevention is central to safety engineering.
ENGR 430 touches quality and reliability, but there is no course dedicated to quantitative reliability modeling or risk quantification, a standard component of a modern safety-engineering credential.

emergingTrends.rationale: ENGR 330 (microcontrollers and IoT) and ENGR 301/CS 301 (data science) are already in the pool and could feed a predictive safety-monitoring elective that reflects the shift to instrumented, real-time hazard detection.

emergingTrends.trend: Predictive maintenance and prognostics with machine learning
emergingTrends.rationale: CS 301 Introduction to Data Science and ENGR 301 could anchor a safety-focused predictive-maintenance course, matching industry's move toward ML-driven failure prediction.

emergingTrends.trend: Functional safety of autonomous and robotic systems
emergingTrends.rationale: With ENGR 423 Drone Science, ENGR 424 Robotics, and ECE 431/432 control systems in the pool, safe operation of autonomous machinery (human-robot collaboration safety) is an emerging safety concern the minor could address.

emergingTrends.trend: Environmental, health and safety (EHS) and sustainability
emergingTrends.rationale: Growing regulatory and ESG pressure makes an EHS/sustainability emphasis, connected to CHE 312 process safety, an increasingly expected part of safety education.
Controls courses (ECE 431/432) are present; a standards-based functional-safety module would align the minor with automotive and industrial-controls hiring expectations.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's required/elective structure and total credits, which are entirely absent (requirementGroups empty; all credit fields null).

Curate the course pool to safety-relevant courses and remove the mis-scoped ones (BME signal processing, CS 440 computer vision, ECE circuits, ENGR 423/424 drones and robotics, ENTR entrepreneurship) that appear pulled from a shared engineering-technology pool.
Add a dedicated hazard-analysis/risk-assessment course (HAZOP, FMEA, fault-tree) - the defining competency of safety engineering, currently absent from the pool.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the safety-engineering minor certifies.
Broaden coverage beyond construction and chemical-process safety to general occupational safety, fire protection, and human-factors/ergonomics.
